title: Chapter 55: The Tower
content: The tower screamed.

Not with sound — with light. Every fixture on floors one through fifteen strobed in alternating patterns, white-red-white-red, a seizure-inducing pulse that drove the security teams into chaos. The elevators cycled between floors without stopping, their doors opening and closing on empty shafts. The fire suppression system activated on floor seven and deactivated on floor eight, alternating every ninety seconds.
Wren was the building's nightmare.

She sat cross-legged in a maintenance hub on floor twenty, surrounded by conduit and humming transformers, her awareness threaded through every circuit in the sixty-floor monolith. Her body was here — small, still, breathing. Her mind was everywhere.
The assault had begun seventeen minutes ago.
Ash went in first.

Not through the lobby — that was a kill zone, wide open and bristling with sensors. He entered through the loading dock on the sublevel, where delivery trucks brought supplies three times a week. The door was password-protected, biometric-locked, and monitored by two cameras. Wren handled all three.

The password was a twelve-character alphanumeric string that rotated weekly. She plucked it from the security server's active memory. The biometric lock required a palm print — she fed it a stored image from the facility manager's access log. The cameras received looped footage of an empty corridor that she'd recorded during her reconnaissance.

The door opened. Ash stepped through. Three other invisibles followed — Rook from Toronto, a former systems analyst who moved through locked doors like they were suggestions; Keiko from Osaka, whose amplification had given her a physical speed that verged on supernatural; and a man named Gabriel from Bogotá, silent and steady, who had spent two years invisible inside a military compound and knew how to move through hostile territory.

The sublevel was clear. Wren had already blinded the cameras and corrupted the motion sensors, feeding them looped footage of an empty corridor. The air down here was thick with detergent and machine oil — the loading dock still smelled of the morning's deliveries.
"Sublevel secure," Ash said through the frequency. His voice was calm. It was always calm.
"Moving to stairwell A," Wren replied. "I've unlocked it. Security team on floor three — four guards, rotating east. You have a ninety-second window."
She felt them move. Not running — that would trigger the audio sensors she couldn't fully mask. A controlled, silent advance. Four predators in a building full of prey that couldn't see them coming.

Floors one through five were administrative — cubicle farms and conference rooms, potted plants and motivational posters. Irrelevant. Wren locked every door on these floors and redirected the HVAC to pump cold air into the corridors, dropping the temperature enough to make the guards uncomfortable and slow. Small advantages.
Floor six was the first checkpoint. A biometric gate spanning the full width of the corridor, flanked by armed security. Two guards. One at the gate, one patrolling.

Wren glitched the gate open. The guard at the station frowned at his terminal, which now displayed a cheerful "SYSTEM UPDATE IN PROGRESS" message that she'd written three days ago. He reached for his radio.

She killed the radio. Every radio on floors six through fifteen simultaneously emitted a burst of high-frequency static that made the guards rip their earpieces out. By the time the checkpoint guard looked up, Ash was already past him — a shift in the air pressure, a faint ozone trace, and then nothing.

Rook phased through the gate itself, his body briefly sharing the same space as the metal bars. Keiko vaulted. Gabriel simply walked around, using the service corridor that Wren had unlocked two minutes earlier.
The guard never knew they were there.
Floor ten was where it got complicated.
The MK.II Hounds.

Wren felt them before the assault team encountered them — a different frequency, sharp and predatory, moving through the building's lower levels. The sensation was like hearing a frequency that shouldn't exist, the glitch turning against itself. Two of them, patrolling in a staggered pattern that covered the entire floor. Their footsteps were hydraulic whispers against the carpet.

Prototypes. Their AI cores were already processing, already predicting, already narrowing the probability fields. The Hounds moved with mechanical precision — sweeping corridors, pausing at intersections, processing data from the building's sensor network in real time.
She hit them with everything she had.

The Hounds' internal navigation systems relied on the building's network for real-time spatial mapping. Wren corrupted the data — fed them false coordinates, phantom heat signatures, impossible geometries. The first Hound stopped in the middle of a corridor and pivoted three hundred and sixty degrees, recalculating. The second moved toward a stairwell that didn't exist.

But they were learning. She could feel them adapting, building new models, compensating for the corrupted data. The first Hound had already begun cross-referencing its internal gyroscope against the network's spatial data, identifying contradictions. The second was switching to infrared scanning, no longer trusting the building's inputs.
Within minutes they'd have enough independent processing to function without the network.
"Ash. Hounds on floor ten. They're adapting faster than I expected."
"How long?"
"Three minutes before they're fully autonomous. Maybe less."
"Then we go through them."

She felt him accelerate. The frequency between them blazed — Ash pushing his amplification to its limit, his form shredding at the edges, moving through the physical world like a blade through water. He hit the stairwell at a dead sprint, his body flickering between solid and glitched, feet barely touching the steps.

The first Hound detected him at twelve meters. Its AI core fired a prediction — trajectory, speed, probable destination — and its combat systems engaged. A mechanical arm swung toward the doorway where Ash would arrive in 0.8 seconds.
Except Ash didn't arrive. He phased through the wall.

The Hound's prediction was wrong for the first time in its operational history. It pivoted, recalculating, but Ash was already inside its guard — his hand closing around the thing's sensor array, his frequency disrupting its targeting systems at the source. Sparks showered from the Hound's neck joint.

Keiko came from above. She'd climbed the wall — actually climbed it, her amplified speed allowing her to find grips in the drywall that no human hand should have been able to hold. She dropped onto the Hound's back, blade first, and severed the primary power conduit that ran along its spine. The thing buckled, hydraulic legs collapsing, and hit the carpet with a sound like a dropped typewriter.

The second Hound adapted. It had watched its partner fall and updated its model. It predicted Keiko's attack pattern and countered, driving a mechanical arm toward her torso with force that would have shattered a visible person's ribs. Keiko twisted — not fast enough to avoid it entirely, the impact catching her shoulder and spinning her sideways. She hit the wall with a grunt.

But Gabriel was already behind it. He'd been running parallel to the corridor, phased into the wall itself, and now he reached through the plaster and closed his fingers around the Hound's power supply. The casing was tough — military-grade polymer — but Gabriel's amplification let him push through matter like it was suggestion. His hand passed through the casing. Found the battery. Squeezed.

Rook appeared through the opposite wall, his hands inside the Hound's torso, rerouting its internal wiring with the same ease he'd once rerouted corporate networks. He pulled. Something vital disconnected.
Three seconds. The Hound collapsed in a shower of sparks and the acrid stench of burning circuitry. The carpet smoldered where the hydraulic fluid had leaked.

Wren exhaled. Her hands were shaking. The building's systems pressed against her awareness — a thousand individual processes demanding attention, security protocols trying to reassert themselves, automated defenses activating on floors she hadn't reached yet.
She pushed harder.

Floors fifteen through twenty-five were research levels. Laboratories, server rooms, cold storage units filled with biological samples. Wren sealed every door and killed the power to the electronic locks, trapping the night-shift researchers in their labs. They were civilians — she had no quarrel with them, and she wouldn't risk them stumbling into the assault team's path.

Floor twenty-three was a problem. A secondary security station with three guards and a hardwired alarm that couldn't be glitched — it ran on a separate circuit, air-gapped from the building's network. Old-school. Effective.
"Ash. Floor twenty-three has an analog alarm. I can't touch it."
"Understood." A pause. "Gabriel?"
"On it."

Gabriel moved through the walls of floor twenty-three like a shadow through water. The guards never heard him coming. He emerged from the plaster behind the alarm station, placed his hand over the pull switch, and held it. Not violence — just presence. An invisible hand over the alarm, ready to pin it the moment someone reached for it.
No one reached. The guards, unable to reach their radios and confused by the building's malfunctioning systems, stayed at their posts and waited for orders that would never come.
Floor thirty. Thirty-five. Forty.

The building was a war zone now, but a war zone that only one side could see. Wren corrupted communications — security teams couldn't coordinate because their earpieces spat static and phantom voices. Locks failed on doors that were supposed to be impenetrable. The climate control systems on floor thirty-five pumped superheated air into a corridor, driving guards into the stairwells where Ash's team was ascending. On floor thirty-eight, she reversed the elevator motors, sending three cars to the basement and locking them there.

Floor forty-five. The resistance thickened. Human guards now — heavily armed, desperate, shooting at shadows. A bullet clipped the wall six inches from Rook's head, and he flinched, the first sign of fear she'd felt from him all night. Another bullet passed through the space where Keiko had been a fraction of a second before, embedding itself in the drywall with a sharp crack.
But they couldn't hit what they couldn't see.

Ash moved through the guards like wind through grass — not attacking, not engaging, just passing. His frequency disrupted their aim at the neural level, their brains glitching for milliseconds at a time, just enough to make their shots fly wide. Keiko disarmed two of them before they knew she was there. Gabriel simply walked past, a ghost in plain sight.

Floor fifty. Fifty-five. The executive levels — polished wood, original art, the smell of expensive coffee and terror. The carpets here were thick enough to muffle footsteps, and the lighting was warm and amber, designed to make power feel comfortable. Wren hated it.

She reached through the network and killed the lights. Every fixture on floors fifty through sixty went dark. The executives' comfortable amber glow vanished, replaced by the red pulse of emergency lighting.
Floor fifty-eight. Vance's office was two floors above them.

Wren pulled her awareness back into her body for a moment. The maintenance hub was hot — the transformers were overloading from the strain she'd put them through. Sweat ran down her spine. The metallic taste of adrenaline coated her tongue. Her fingers ached from gripping nothing.

She could still feel the building around her — every circuit, every wire, every glowing screen. It was hers. All of it. A sixty-floor instrument that she played like a piano, and every note was chaos.
She reached for Ash through the frequency.
"I'll meet you up there."
"Stay in the systems. I'll handle the physical approach."
"No. This one's mine."

She stood. Her legs protested — she'd been sitting for forty minutes in a position that would have been uncomfortable even without the mental strain. She stretched, feeling her shoulders crack, feeling the ache in her lower back where the concrete floor had pressed against her spine. The building's systems hummed in her peripheral awareness, still hers, still controlled.

Two floors. One elevator shaft — she'd already cleared it, the car dispatched to the basement and the emergency brakes engaged so it couldn't be recalled. One stairwell — she'd locked the doors behind her so no one could follow.

The stairwell was dark. Emergency lights cast red pools on the concrete. Wren climbed quickly, her hand trailing along the railing, feeling the metal cool beneath her fingers. The air was warmer up here — the executive floors had their own climate system, and the heat was still running despite the lights being out.

She could hear voices from floor fifty-nine — security, sounding frantic, calling for backup that would never arrive. She could hear the whine of a generator on the roof, the hum of the building's data center two floors below. She could hear her own heartbeat, steady and strong, and beneath it, the frequency — the glitch singing in her blood like a second pulse.
Fifty-ninth floor. The door was locked. She glitched through it, her body becoming data for a fraction of a second, passing through the metal and glass and emerging on the other side.

The corridor was empty. Polished wood paneling. Art on the walls — abstract pieces that probably cost more than her mother's house. The air smelled of leather and sandalwood and something chemical underneath — the Counter-Signal. Close now. Very close.

Fifty-ninth floor was the research level. The Counter-Signal device sat in the center of a lab, half-assembled, its transducer array exposed like an open wound. Technicians had fled when the alarms started — she could see their coffee cups still steaming on a workbench, their tablets abandoned on chairs.
Wren reached out through the frequency and killed the device. Not dramatically — she just severed the power coupling. The amber light on its surface flickered and died. Dead metal. Harmless.
She kept walking.
Wren stepped into the elevator shaft and glitched upward through the darkness, rising through the building she'd already conquered, toward the woman at its apex.
The tower shuddered around her. Not from damage — from the weight of what was coming.
Sixty floors of secrets. And Wren was coming for all of them.
